ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a mixed Thai snack platter. FOODON_03315091 (fried chicken wing food product) is used as the closest parent anchor for the dominant fried protein component. The meatball and crispy fish components are not individually represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dishes — mixed meat and fish snack platter
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (composite dishes) is the closest defensible parent for a mixed fried snack platter containing meatballs, chicken wings, and crispy fish. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific Thai combination.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept maps to this specific mixed Thai snack platter.

serving.description: 1 box (4 pcs meatballs + 4 pcs chicken wing & drumette + 6 pcs crispy fish)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is one Snack Delight Box B, comprising 4 pieces of beef or chicken meatballs, 4 pieces of chicken wings and drumettes, and 6 pieces of crispy fried fish, estimated at approximately 350 g total.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods: Chicken wing fried (NDB 05062), Fish fillet fried breaded (NDB 15209), Meatball beef/chicken fried (SR Legacy), weighted by approximate portion mass
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific Thai mixed snack platter. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted average of three USDA SR Legacy component foods: fried chicken wing (~140 g, 40% of serving), fried breaded fish fillet (~120 g, 34% of serving), and fried beef/chicken meatball (~90 g, 26% of serving). Per-100 g values are derived from this composite. FAO/INFOODS SEA tables were also consulted for Thai fried chicken and fish fritter analogues to cross-validate protein and fat ranges. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed fried meat and fish snack estimated from analogous foods: fried chicken (GI ~40, Atkinson et al. 2008) and fried fish cake (GI ~38–52, University of Sydney GI Database); meatball component estimated at GI ~45 based on protein-fat dominant composition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific Thai mixed snack platter. GI of 45 is estimated as a weighted average of analogous foods from the University of Sydney GI Database: fried chicken GI ~40 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281), fish cake GI ~45 (University of Sydney GI Database), and fried meatball GI ~50 (protein-fat dominant, low-carbohydrate composition). Weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution of each component. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 30.1) / 100 ≈ 13.5, rounded to 13.3 using refined carbohydrate estimate of 29.6 g per 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this platter due to its very high sodium content (~1680 mg per serving, exceeding 70% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg), primarily from seasoned meatballs, fried fish coatings, and Thai marinades; portion control and pairing with low-sodium foods is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~13 per serving) and high fat content from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: This platter has a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~45) and moderate glycemic load (~13 per serving), making acute blood glucose impact manageable, but the high total fat content (~46 g per serving) from deep-frying may contribute to insulin resistance and excess caloric intake; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and frequency of consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium from deep-frying and processed meat components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This platter is high in saturated fat (~3.4 g/100 g), contains trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), and delivers substantial sodium (~1680 mg per serving) and cholesterol (~252 mg per serving), all of which are established cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional food and prioritise grilled or steamed protein alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Thai Snack Platter (Snack Delight Box B) is a mixed Thai-inspired fried snack combination served at Saap Saap Thai restaurant in Singapore. One box contains four pieces of beef or chicken meatballs (luk chin), four pieces of fried chicken wings and drumettes, and six pieces of crispy fried fish, totalling approximately 350 g per serving. The meatballs are seasoned with Thai herbs and spices, the chicken wings are marinated in Thai-style sauces and deep-fried to a golden crisp, and the fish pieces are coated in a light batter and fried until crunchy. The overall flavour profile is savoury, umami-rich, and mildly spiced, with contrasting textures of juicy meatballs, crispy chicken skin, and crunchy fish. Nutritionally, this platter is energy-dense (~218 kcal per 100 g, ~763 kcal per serving), high in protein (~16.5 g/100 g) from the meat and fish components, and high in fat (~13.2 g/100 g) due to deep-frying. Sodium content is high (~480 mg/100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at ~45 (low), reflecting the protein- and fat-dominant composition with moderate carbohydrate from batter coatings. This dish is suitable as a shared appetiser or snack in a Thai meal context and is commonly ordered for group dining or casual sharing occasions in Singapore's Thai restaurant segment.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific Thai mixed snack platter sold at Saap Saap Thai Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of three USDA SR Legacy component foods (fried chicken wing, fried breaded fish fillet, fried meatball), cross-validated against FAO/INFOODS SEA analogues. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried chicken and fish cake. Serving size (350 g) is estimated from typical Thai snack platter portion norms. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Serving size of 350 g is estimated based on typical Thai mixed snack platter portion norms (4 meatballs ~90 g, 4 chicken wing/drumette pieces ~140 g, 6 crispy fish pieces ~120 g). Actual restaurant portion may vary. 2. Halal status is listed as false/unknown; Saap Saap Thai is not confirmed halal-certified as of the processing date. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for estimation. 4. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for estimation. 5. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for estimation. 6. Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) is estimated from the deep-frying process using vegetable oil; actual value depends on oil type and frying conditions. 7. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 8. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible parent for composite mixed meat and fish dishes; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for this Thai snack platter. 9. Allergen list includes likely allergens based on typical Thai meatball and fried fish batter formulations; actual allergen content should be confirmed with the restaurant.
